food_name: Png Kueh (Steamed Glutinous Rice Cake with Savoury Filling)
region_of_origin: Singapore (Teochew and Hokkien Chinese heritage; origins in Fujian and Chaoshan regions of China)
food_subgroup: Steamed glutinous rice flour cake with savoury peanut-bamboo shoot-dried shrimp filling, 1 piece ~80g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Png Kueh as a prepared Singaporean hawker food. Closest parent would be a generic steamed rice cake concept; no exact URI available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed glutinous rice cake with savoury filling (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Png Kueh)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KDA is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a steamed glutinous rice cake. No exact code exists for this specific Teochew/Hokkien hawker preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Png Kueh as a specific prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of Png Kueh is 1 piece (~80g), typically flower- or tortoise-shaped, consisting of a steamed glutinous rice flour shell encasing a savoury bamboo shoot, peanut, and dried shrimp filling.

nutrient_source.title: Png Kueh — HPB Singapore Foodo Database + USDA FoodData Central Component Calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Png Kueh as the primary source, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central component-level data for glutinous rice flour, peanuts, bamboo shoots, and dried shrimp to complete micronutrient profile. Values represent a composite calculation for the standard 80g hawker piece.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Glutinous Rice-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~72 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous (waxy) rice, which is composed of 100% amylopectin with zero amylose. Fully gelatinised during steaming, resulting in rapid starch digestion and a GI higher than regular white rice (GI ~64–72). The peanut filling partially moderates the glycaemic response via fat-mediated slowing of gastric emptying. GL per 80g piece = (72 × 32g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 23.0; input record states 20.9 using slightly different available carbohydrate estimate. Value of 20.9 retained from HPB-sourced calculation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~300mg per 80g piece, primarily from dried shrimp (hei bi) in the filling
hypertension.guidance: One piece of Png Kueh contributes approximately 300mg sodium (~13% of the 2,300mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit to 1 piece per occasion and monitor total daily sodium intake from other meals, as 2 pieces would contribute ~600mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from glutinous rice (100% amylopectin), with glycemic load ~21 per piece
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glutinous rice has a HIGH GI (~72), higher than regular white rice, because 100% amylopectin starch is fully gelatinised during steaming and digests rapidly; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to 1 piece per occasion and pair with low-GI foods, as 2 pieces would produce a combined GL of ~42, which is clinically significant for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8g per 100g; ~1.4g per piece) and moderate sodium; peanut filling provides MUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Png Kueh has a relatively low saturated fat content (~1.4g per 80g piece) and zero trans fat, making it acceptable for cardiovascular health in moderation; however, the moderate sodium (~300mg per piece) warrants caution for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Png Kueh (also known as Ku Kueh or Ang Ku Kueh) is a traditional Singaporean Teochew and Hokkien steamed glutinous rice cake, typically shaped like a flower or tortoise (~80g per piece). The outer shell is made from glutinous rice flour (100% amylopectin), which is steamed until translucent and chewy. The savoury filling consists of bamboo shoots, ground peanuts, and dried shrimp (hei bi), seasoned with salt and aromatics. The texture is soft, sticky, and slightly chewy on the outside with a dense, savoury, nutty filling. Nutritionally, one piece (~80g) provides approximately 195 kcal, 4g protein, 6g fat, and 32g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is HIGH (~72) — higher than regular white rice — because glutinous rice contains 100% amylopectin with zero amylose, which is fully gelatinised during steaming and digests very rapidly. This is a critical clinical distinction: glutinous rice is NOT lower GI than regular rice. Sodium per piece is approximately 300mg, primarily from dried shrimp. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but is NOT halal (contains dried shrimp; may use lard glaze in traditional preparations). Culturally, Png Kueh originates from the Fujian and Chaoshan regions of China and was brought to Singapore by Teochew and Hokkien immigrants. It is traditionally made for festivals and ancestor offerings but is now a common everyday hawker snack. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central component calculation.
meal_context: temple offering adapted to everyday snack

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) supplemented by USDA FoodData Central component-level data. GI value of ~72 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous (waxy) rice. All values are consistent with the known composition of glutinous rice flour, peanuts, bamboo shoots, and dried shrimp. Confidence is high for macronutrients and GI; micronutrient values are component-calculated and carry slightly lower individual certainty but are collectively consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Png Kueh preparation is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish; individual fatty acid data would require full recipe-level disaggregation. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific steamed glutinous rice cake preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source; no hydrogenated fats used in standard hawker preparation. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based steamed cake with minimal animal-derived ingredients (dried shrimp contributes negligible vitamin D). Halal status: NOT halal in traditional preparations — dried shrimp (crustacean) is present, and some traditional recipes use lard glaze; hawker stall preparations vary. GI note: glutinous rice (100% amylopectin) has a HIGHER GI than regular white rice — this is a clinically important distinction for diabetes management. GL per piece calculated as (72 × 32g available carbohydrate per 80g piece) / 100 ≈ 23.0; the value of 20.9 from the input record is retained as it reflects the HPB-sourced available carbohydrate estimate of ~29g.
